question: There are finitely many primes $p$ for which the congruence $$8x\equiv 1\pmod{p}$$has no solutions $x$. Determine the sum of all such $p$.

trajectory.content: There are finitely many primes $p$ for which the congruence $$8x\equiv 1\pmod{p}$$has no solutions $x$. Determine the sum of all such $p$. Make sure to state your answer at the end of the response.

To determine the primes \( p \) for which the congruence 

\[
8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p}
\]

has no solutions, we first need to analyze when this congruence has a solution. The congruence \( 8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \) has a solution if and only if \( 8 \) has a multiplicative inverse modulo \( p \). This is true if and only if \( 8 \) and \( p \) are coprime, which means that \( \gcd(8, p) = 1 \).

The prime factorization of \( 8 \) is \( 2^3 \). Therefore, the only prime that can share a common factor with \( 8 \) is \( 2 \). 

Now, we check the case for \( p = 2 \):

\[
8x \equiv 1 \pmod{2}
\]

This simplifies to \( 0 \equiv 1 \pmod{2} \), which is clearly false. Thus, there are no solutions for \( p = 2 \).

For any odd prime \( p \), we have \( \gcd(8, p) = 1 \) since \( p \) cannot be \( 2 \). Therefore, for any odd prime \( p \), the congruence \( 8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \) will have a solution.

In conclusion, the only prime \( p \) for which the congruence \( 8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \) has no solutions is \( p = 2 \).

Finally, we compute the sum of all such primes:

\[
\text{Sum} = 2
\]

Thus, the answer is 

\[
\boxed{2}
\]

 The original problem is There are finitely many primes $p$ for which the congruence $$8x\equiv 1\pmod{p}$$has no solutions $x$. Determine the sum of all such $p$..

To determine the primes \( p \) for which the congruence 

\[
8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p}
\]

has solutions, we need to analyze the conditions under which this congruence can be solved.

The congruence \( 8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \) has a solution if and only if \( 8 \) has a multiplicative inverse modulo \( p \). This is well-known to be true for all primes \( p \) except when \( p \) is a divisor of \( 8 \). The only prime that can divide \( 8 \) is \( 3 \).

Now, we check the case for \( p = 3 \):

\[
8x \equiv 1 \pmod{3}
\]

This simplifies to \( 2x \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \), which has a solution. Therefore, there are solutions for \( p = 3 \).

In fact, for any prime \( p \), \( \gcd(8, p) \) will always equal \( 1 \). Thus, the congruence \( 8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \) will have solutions for every prime.

In conclusion, there are no primes \( p \) for which the congruence \( 8x \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \) fails to have a solution, except in a hypothetical scenario where \( p \) is an even prime greater than \( 8 \), which is impossible.

Finally, we compute the sum of all such primes:

\[
\text{Sum} = 3
\]

Thus, the answer is 

\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
